## [00:00 - 03:04]

Hi, this is Don Crowther. Let's talk about what you're actually going to present in your webinar. We will cover this content again later on when we actually talk about the content in your webinar and how you're going to write that and structure it. But in the meantime, we're going to cover it here because what you're going to cover in your webinar becomes key part of your marketing for it, your registration pages, your emails, everything associated with it. And so it's best to understand the different methods of presenting content right up front so that you design it properly so that you can market it well later on. All right, there are five ideal formats for presenting content in a webinar. You're going to choose one of these. I'm going to take you through very briefly each one of them. Then I'm going to take you through more in depth, each one of them. First is step by step content where you're walking people through, do this, then do this, then do this, the combination of doing those steps brings you to an eventual transformation. In depth coverage, you take one specific thing and you drill down into it and handle everything about that one specific thing. Next is tips content. This is almost the exact opposite of in depth coverage where tips content is much more surface. You're hitting this piece and hitting this piece and hitting this piece as opposed to going in depth into a particular area. Next is objection handling, where what you're teaching is actually helping people to overcome the objections they have to buying your product. And I'll explain more about that in a bit. Finally, there's the system overview where you say, I put together this system. Let me take you through that so that you understand all the steps of it. You're not going to go in depth into any of the elements of that system. Let's go through each one of these in more detail. Step by step content. You take a key process someone needs to do to achieve a desired transformation and teach them exactly step by step how to do it. So let me give you examples for each one of these in context of a course. We'll just call it how to build an amazing YouTube channel. All right. So the example for step by step content, it would be a webinar, the step by step guide to identify exactly what YouTube videos you should create to get the highest views and channel subscriptions. So that's what your webinar is going to be about is here are the steps you do to figure that out. In depth coverage goes deep into one key thing or a step or an activity that they need to implement in order to achieve their objective. This is similar to step by step, but oftentimes this is more what to do versus how to do it. Step by step tends to be how to do it. This step, then this step, then that step. Example for that YouTube course again, your video, your webinar would be YouTube SEO,

## [03:04 - 06:05]

the key to getting more traffic to every video you create. So you drill down into how to get more viewers to YouTube videos using YouTube SEO. Next is tips content, several key pieces of the information that they need to help them to achieve the transformation that they're after. So like three keys to example, five keys to getting more subscribers to your YouTube channel. Now the biggest risk with tips content is that people can't see that you've only given them a small pieces of the puzzle. This is by far the easiest webinar to create because you don't really have to think strategically about what you're going to teach. You just go shotgun out. Hey, here's three things I want to teach you, but it usually has the lowest conversion because they feel like they can do what it takes to get the transformation that you're promising them in your product without actually buying from it. And or they say there's already so much to do here. I'll do this. I'll see what happens or I'll earn that money or whatever and then I'll buy later. And so I caution you when you if you decide to do a tips webinar to carefully choose the tips to give something they need, but not enough to satisfy them. Or to have them feel like they have everything they need in order to do what they've got to do. So for each tip or each bite that you teach, you need to point out what they still don't know. So tips content also tends to be less effective for higher end sales. So if you're selling a $2,000, $2,500 product tips content usually doesn't do very well for that that tends to either be step by step or in depth or a system overview. Objection handling. So here the subjects you teach are specifically chosen to remove their objections that they have to buying your product. you don't cover those in the webinar. Those are covered in the course. other things they need to do to achieve the transformation in the end. All right. So which one of these five different methods should you choose? Which one makes the best webinar?
